dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a Sick Person at a hotel in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
Our social worker contacted 911 because the patient wanted to go to the hospital.
Can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
He can't respond verbally. He's hard of hearing and communicates through writing.
Can you push up onto the chair for me to check some movement? Let's try now.
He is awake, with eyes open and looking around.
He appears alert and oriented.
Do you have any trouble breathing or feel like your throat is blocked? Please write if you can.

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Respiration rate is 20, even and regular with no signs of distress.

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: SpO₂ is 98% on room air.
Pulse is 131, strong and regular with easy palpation in wrist and neck.
Blood pressure is 166 over 110 millimeters of mercury.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry, with no mottling or poor perfusion.
Peripheral pulses are strong and consistent in both upper and lower extremities.
Can you write that you know where you are and why you're calling for help?
Can you tell me what you're feeling or experiencing right now, or is it something you mentioned to your social worker that brought this on today?
He has diabetes and requires insulin for management. No reported other medical history today.
He told his social worker he wanted to go to the hospital but was not experiencing pain or symptoms at the time.
You do not have any known drug allergies, correct? Let us confirm before we move forward.
I am unsure of the last time he ate, but he usually eats regularly as part of managing his diabetes.
We know you're currently managing with insulin. Are there any other medications or supplements?
Can you write to confirm that you're not experiencing pain or discomfort at this time?
Skin is warm to the touch across all areas examined.
We will observe and document your pupil size and reactivity to light now.
Head and neck show no signs of trauma, deformity, or visible masses.
Can you give permission to look at your ears, nose, and mouth for drainage or blockage?
Mouth is clear with no inflammation or sores visible.
I am going to place the stethoscope on your chest to listen to your breathing now.
Lung sounds are clear in all lobes with equal chest movement.
Blood glucose is currently 341 mg/dL.
Let's perform a 4-lead ECG now for monitoring purposes.
The ECG returned a result of sinus tachycardia.
4-lead ECG setup complete and ready for continual monitoring.
Based on hyperglycemia levels and your diabetes history, we are following the diabetic hyperglycemia protocol.
Respiratory rate remains at 20 breaths per minute, regular, and no distress.
Blood pressure is 160 over 119 millimeters of mercury.
Pulse is now 129 beats per minute, strong and consistent.
SpO₂ is 100% on room air.
We are going to start an IV in your arm to help with fluid administration.
IV line placed with a 20-gauge catheter in your left hand.
250 mL of normal saline is being administered IV as per protocol.
Pulse remains at 129 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is still 20, regular and no signs of distress.
Blood pressure is 160 over 119 millimeters of mercury.
SpO₂ is 100% with continued good oxygenation.
IV site is functioning properly, no signs of swelling or infiltration.
ECG monitor is functioning normally with stable readouts.
Pulse has slightly decreased to 123 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 174 over 90 now, showing changes in pressure.
SpO₂ is 97% and still within acceptable parameters.
We will transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation.
Placing the patient safely on the stretcher now with a 5-point harness.
Patient is now fully secured and ready to proceed to the ambulance.
Patient was transferred onto the ED bed and passed over to the ED nurse with your reports.
